label: Uneasy calm
summary: The world held at its uneasy-calm reading of 41 as the direct US-Iran fighting that dominated the week appeared to pause, even as its wreckage kept weighing on the ledger. After two days of exchanged strikes, open attacks quieted while Pakistan and Qatar pressed both capitals back toward talks; Washington says negotiations continue though it still calls the ceasefire 'over.' The cost of the flare-up lingered in the Strait of Hormuz, where tanker traffic slowed to near zero after the strikes, keeping a risk premium on oil with Brent near $76 a barrel. Markets nonetheless read the calm as the stronger signal: the S&P 500 rose 0.42 percent to 7,575, the Nasdaq added 0.29 percent and the Dow edged up, led by SK Hynix's record $26.5bn US debut, though a soft US June jobs report of 57,000 tempered the mood. Elsewhere the news ran heavy. One of Spain's deadliest wildfires killed at least 12 people in Almeria with 23 missing, many caught fleeing along a dry riverbed. Super Typhoon Bavi lashed Taiwan at peak strength, shutting work and schools islandwide before an expected evening landfall on China's Fujian-Zhejiang coast. DR Congo's Ebola death toll passed 600 with suspected cases spreading to new provinces, Sudan's RSF held half a million civilians under siege in El Obeid, and Venezuela stayed in mourning as its twin-quake toll climbed past 3,500. Against that ran real lift: Oxford's Sir Adrian Hill won the European Inventor Award for the low-cost R21 malaria vaccine credited with saving tens of thousands, and Fijian families displaced by Cyclone Ana finally moved into permanent homes. The headline settles at 41, steadied by a pause in the fighting and market calm but capped by deadly weather, worsening outbreaks and a still-shut shipping lane.

drivers.headline: Direct US-Iran attacks appear to pause after two days of exchanged strikes, as mediators Pakistan and Qatar press both capitals back toward negotiations and Washington says talks with Tehran will continue even while it maintains that the ceasefire is 'over,' offering the first clear de-escalation since the interim deal frayed earlier in the week

Nine months into the US-brokered ceasefire, Israel's military now controls nearly 70 percent of Gaza after pushing deeper into the enclave including the Shujaiya area, with the UN humanitarian office counting around 200 Palestinians killed by Israeli forces near shifting front lines since the truce began, as President Trump's peace plan calling for Israeli withdrawal, new governance and Hamas disarmament remains stalled

US stocks close higher on Friday to cap a choppy week, with the S&P 500 gaining 0.42 percent to 7,575.39, the Nasdaq Composite rising 0.29 percent to 26,281.61 and the Dow Jones Industrial Average adding 0.28 percent to 52,637.01, as chipmaker SK Hynix soars about 13 percent in a record-breaking $26.5 billion Wall Street debut that tested the AI memory trade while investors monitored the US-Iran de-escalation

Commercial tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz slows to near zero after this week's US-Iran strikes, choking a waterway that normally carries roughly a fifth of the world's oil and gas and keeping a war-risk premium on crude prices, with Brent settling near $76 a barrel, about $4 above pre-crisis levels and up around 5 percent on the week, as Gulf producers strain to sustain exports around the disruption

The US labour market shows fresh signs of cooling as employers added just 57,000 jobs in June, less than half of May's gain and well short of the 115,000 economists expected, with downward revisions to April and May and an unemployment rate ticking to 4.2 percent largely because fewer people were looking for work, undercutting claims of a booming economy

One of Spain's deadliest wildfires on record kills at least 12 people overnight in the southern province of Almeria with 23 others unaccounted for, as blazes fanned by soaring temperatures near the Sierra de los Filabres trap victims in burnt-out vehicles, several believed to have died trying to flee down a dry riverbed that turned into a death trap after ignoring shelter-in-place orders

Super Typhoon Bavi delivers its strongest blow to Taiwan on Friday, with all cities and counties except Taitung and Kinmen declaring a typhoon day that cancels work and classes as gusts top 118kph along the north coast and east-coast waves reach nine metres, before the storm is forecast to make landfall this evening on China's coast between Fuqing in Fujian and Wenling in Zhejiang, threatening extreme rainfall across the eastern seaboard

The Ebola death toll in the Democratic Republic of the Congo passes 600 with roughly 1,790 confirmed cases in the Bundibugyo-virus outbreak, and the government reports new suspected cases in Tshopo and Haut-Uele provinces previously untouched by the epidemic, signalling continued spread beyond the Ituri epicentre as the WHO warns the outbreak is not yet under control

Sudan's Rapid Support Forces maintain their siege of El Obeid, the capital of North Kordofan, leaving roughly half a million civilians trapped in a city without electricity for nearly a month amid fuel shortages and food prices up as much as 300 percent, as the UN documents ten consecutive days of drone strikes killing at least 50 people and rights groups warn the city could become the next El-Fasher-style atrocity

Venezuela remains in national mourning as the death toll from the June 24 twin earthquakes climbs past 3,500 with more than 16,700 injured and nearly 18,000 left without housing, as survivors keep searching shelters for missing relatives more than two weeks after back-to-back magnitude-7.2 and 7.5 quakes flattened much of La Guaira and battered Caracas, with the final toll feared to run higher still

Oxford's Sir Adrian Hill wins the European Patent Office's European Inventor Award 2026 for developing the R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine, which reaches roughly 80 percent protection in trials, costs under three euros a dose and stays stable for up to two years under standard refrigeration, and has been rolled out across around 20 nations in a project credited with preventing tens of thousands of deaths

Fijian families displaced by Tropical Cyclone Ana finally move into permanent homes after nearly six years living in tents, a milestone in the country's long climate-recovery effort, as Canberra pledges more than one billion Australian dollars in Pacific support over the coming decade and New Zealand weighs joining the Australia-Fiji Ocean of Peace security pact

sources_consulted: Wikipedia (2026 Iran war / 2026 Iran war ceasefire / 2026 Strait of Hormuz crisis / Typhoon Bavi 2026 / 2026 Venezuela earthquakes / 2026 Ebola epidemic / Gaza war / 2026 Iranian supreme leader election / Timeline of the Russo-Ukrainian war)
